name: Microsoft Kinect
short_description: A controller-free motion-sensing peripheral for the Xbox 360 that tracks bodies, gestures, and voice using an RGB camera, infrared depth sensor, and microphone array.
long_description: Kinect is a sensor bar that sits beneath a television and uses a structured-light infrared depth camera, an RGB camera, and a microphone array to reconstruct players' skeletons in 3D and recognize their gestures and speech, letting them control games and menus without a handheld controller. Released in 2010 for the Xbox 360, it became the fastest-selling consumer electronics device on record and, beyond gaming, put affordable real-time depth sensing into the hands of researchers and hobbyists. That cheap depth perception seeded a wave of physical-AI work in robotics navigation, 3D scanning, and gesture interfaces, making it a key ancestor of room-scale ambient sensing in everyday spaces.

images.caption: The Microsoft Kinect sensor bar for Xbox 360, showing its RGB camera, infrared depth sensor, and microphone array.
images.alt_text: A horizontal black Microsoft Kinect sensor bar with multiple camera and sensor lenses across its front, mounted on a small motorized base.

sources.title: Kinect
sources.access_notes: Overview of Kinect hardware, release date (November 4, 2010), depth-sensing technology, and sales records.

sources.title: Ten years on, Kinect's legacy goes beyond Xbox
sources.access_notes: Retrospective on Kinect's influence on robotics, 3D scanning, and depth-sensing research beyond gaming.

interactions.title: Become the controller
interactions.description: Stand in the living room and steer a raft down rapids or spike a volleyball by swinging your own arms and legs — the sensor reads your whole skeleton, no controller in hand.

interactions.title: Wave a room into a 3D model
interactions.description: Walk a Kinect around your office and watch a geometrically accurate 3D mesh of the room and its furniture build up on screen in real time, using only the depth stream.

interactions.title: Cracked open in hours
interactions.description: Within days of the 2010 launch a hacker wrote open-source drivers to pull the raw depth and color feeds off the sensor, claiming Adafruit's bounty and turning a $150 game peripheral into the depth camera researchers couldn't otherwise afford.

interactions.title: Scrub-in gestures over the operating table
interactions.description: Surgeons mount a Kinect in theatre and flip through a patient's scans mid-operation with a wave of a gloved hand, never breaking sterility to touch a keyboard or mouse.

interactions.title: A $20 motion-capture studio
interactions.description: Indie animators stand in front of a secondhand Kinect with tools like Brekel and record their own performance straight onto a 3D character rig, sidestepping a professional mocap stage entirely.

interactions.title: Coach that watches your form
interactions.description: Doing squats or rehab exercises in front of the sensor, get told you're leaning too far forward or not going deep enough — built on the same skeletal joint tracking the fitness games already use.

interactions.title: A room that knows it's empty
interactions.description: Left running on a shelf, the depth camera notices when the last person leaves and how many are present, letting lights and heat idle down without anyone wearing a tag or pressing a switch — grounded in its fixed-position body and motion sensing.
